one_line_takeaway: FaithSCAN detects VQA faithfulness hallucinations from a single VLM forward pass by fusing token-level, visual-patch, and cross-modal-alignment uncertainty signals via attention, matching or beating sampling-based baselines' AUROC (up to +8%) at a fraction of the latency, though cross-dataset generalization remains weak; this is a hallucination-detection/reliability paper with no adversarial or security framing.
key_finding: On in-distribution evaluation (Table III), FaithSCAN matches or exceeds all baselines across nearly every backbone/dataset/metric combination while running in a single forward pass. Representative AUROC numbers: IB-T5-XL backbone -- FaithSCAN 0.754/0.713/0.752 on HalLoc-VQA/HaloQuest/VQA v2 respectively vs. best non-FaithSCAN baseline (Logistic) 0.669/0.735/0.710; LLaVA-8B -- FaithSCAN 0.779/0.827/0.685 vs. Logistic 0.733/0.834/0.592 and SEU 0.769 on HalLoc-VQA; Qwen3-VL-8B -- FaithSCAN 0.739/0.829/0.695 vs. P(True) 0.750/0.631/0.777 (P(True) wins on VQA v2 for this backbone). The paper reports 'up to 8% AUROC improvement' over SOTA on ID settings. Latency: FaithSCAN inference is ~0.6-1.9 seconds per 100 samples with zero extra sampling cost, versus sampling-heavy baselines like SelfCheckGPT (78.6-367.5s inference per 100 samples) and SE/SEU which also require repeated generation (1,257-2,632s sampling time per 100 samples for all methods including FaithSCAN's own sampling-free baseline generation). On OOD evaluation (Table IV, cross-dataset transfer), performance drops substantially and is inconsistent -- e.g. LLaVA-8B trained on HalLoc-VQA and tested on HaloQuest falls to 0.393 AUROC (below random, flagged red in the paper), while multi-source training (all datasets) improves OOD AUROC across the board (e.g. All->HaloQuest reaches 0.696-0.796 depending on backbone). RQ2 shows the model-driven Visual-NLI supervision achieves 0.950-0.990 raw agreement with human annotators and Cohen's Kappa/MCC mostly >0.8 across all four datasets. RQ3 finds token-level uncertainty is consistently the strongest single signal; visual patch uncertainty helps substantially specifically for POPE (predominantly object hallucinations) but contributes little on HalLoc-VQA/VQA v2 (reasoning-heavy); of the token-level signals, hidden embeddings > entropy > log-likelihood individually, and intermediate decoder layers outperform first/last layers.
core_contribution: The paper proposes FaithSCAN, a lightweight supervised classifier that detects faithfulness hallucinations in Visual Question Answering (VQA) from a SINGLE forward pass of a frozen vision-language model (VLM), instead of the repeated-sampling or external-verification pipelines used by prior work. FaithSCAN extracts three heterogeneous internal signal types from the target VLM: (i) token-level generation signals (log-likelihood, predictive entropy, decoder hidden states), (ii) intermediate visual patch embeddings from the vision encoder, and (iii) cross-modal aligned visual-token embeddings. Each signal stream is compressed by an independent 'Branch-wise Evidence Encoding' module into a 64-dim embedding, then combined via an 'Uncertainty-aware Attention' fusion mechanism (cross-branch attention + gated residual) into a hallucination probability via a sigmoid output. A second contribution is a low-cost, model-dependent supervision procedure: the paper extends the LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm into a 'Visual-NLI' protocol, where a judge VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct, T=0.1) scores the target model's generated answer against the gold reference (entailment/contradiction/uncertainty over R judging rounds), producing a binary hallucination label without human annotation. Labels are validated with a small-scale human verification study (100 samples/dataset) and an automated post-hoc reliability-scoring framework.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is not a security paper and has no adversary, attacker capability, or threat model: it studies a reliability/robustness failure mode (naturally occurring hallucination in VLM-generated VQA answers) rather than an attack, defense, or adversarial setting. No adversarial perturbations, no attacker-controlled inputs, and no security property (confidentiality/integrity/availability) is discussed anywhere in the paper.
limitations: The paper's own text surfaces several limitations rather than claiming a fully solved problem. (1) OOD/cross-dataset generalization is weak and inconsistent -- single-source-trained detectors sometimes score below 0.5 AUROC (generalization failure) on unseen hallucination types (e.g., object/scene-trained models transferred to false-premise-heavy HaloQuest), and the authors explicitly note 'a performance gap between ID and OOD underscores the challenge... motivating future work.' (2) The detector is inherently model-dependent -- input dimensionality/extraction points are tied to a specific VLM's architecture, so a new detector must be trained per backbone rather than transferring off-the-shelf. (3) The Visual-NLI supervision signal, while highly agreement with humans on average (0.95-0.99), is shown via a documented failure case (Fig. 3b, VQA v2) to sometimes misjudge minor factual errors as entailment when masked by semantic similarity. (4) Interpretability analysis (RQ4) is limited to text-side token attribution because 'fused visual features are difficult to map back to the original image in an orderly way' -- the authors flag image-side attribution as future work. (5) The reliability-score-weighted training extension (Sec. IV-C3) is described only as 'preliminary' validation deferred to supplementary material, not a fully realized/validated contribution in the main text.

datasets_used: HalLoc-VQA (80,540/10,932/10,082 train/val/test; token-level verification; object + relation/scene hallucination types)
HaloQuest (7,140/608 train/test; answer-level verification; object + partial relation/scene + false-premise types)
VQA v2 (443,757/214,354/447,793 train/val/test; answer-level verification; object + relation/scene types)
quantitative_results: ID AUROC, IB-T5-XL backbone: FaithSCAN 0.754 (HalLoc-VQA) / 0.713 (HaloQuest) / 0.752 (VQA v2)
ID AUROC, LLaVA-8B backbone: FaithSCAN 0.779 (HalLoc-VQA) / 0.827 (HaloQuest) / 0.685 (VQA v2)
ID AUROC, Qwen3-VL-8B backbone: FaithSCAN 0.739 (HalLoc-VQA) / 0.829 (HaloQuest) / 0.695 (VQA v2)
Paper claims up to 8% AUROC improvement over SOTA baselines on in-distribution settings
FaithSCAN inference latency: 0.605-1.898 s per 100 samples across backbones, vs. SelfCheckGPT 78.6-367.5 s per 100 samples inference time
OOD AUROC failure example: LLaVA-8B trained on HalLoc-VQA, tested on HaloQuest = 0.393 (below-chance, flagged as generalization failure)
Multi-source (all-dataset) training improves OOD AUROC, e.g. All->HaloQuest reaches 0.696 (IB-T5-XL) / 0.796 (LLaVA-8B) / 0.749 (Qwen3-VL-8B)
Model-driven Visual-NLI supervision vs. human annotation agreement: 0.950-0.990 raw agreement, Cohen's Kappa 0.798-0.947, MCC 0.799-0.948 across the four datasets
Layer-wise analysis: best decoder layer beats mean-layer AUROC by +0.225 (IB-T5-XL), +0.227 (LLaVA-8B), +0.235 (Qwen3-VL-8B)
